Boat Reviews

Test Article

By Clark Haley · April 17, 2026 · 19 min read

Notes on Nordhavn #5524

Passwords

Aegean AN7000 WIFI Extender: http://192.168.15.1

Aegean Wireless Access Points: 2.4 Ghz SSID: N5524-2.4 Password: Nordhavn5524

5.0 Ghz SSID: N5524-5.0 Password: Nordhavn5524

The Aegean device is located behind the 2nd Ceiling panel in the Captains Quarters

To connect using the http above, make sure your computer is connected to N5524-2.4 or N5524-5.0. Open your favorite web browser and type in the http address exactly as shown above. Once logged on, you can select an WIFI connection as needed. The default will be N5524, which is the Starlink antenna, but you can also choose a marina WIFI network or any other connection you have a password for.

For support, contact: Richard Aguirre @ 754-223-2240 or Rob Cote @561-281-1626

Pepwave Max Transit: http://192.168.50.1 User: Admin Password: Nordhavn5524

This device is located behind the 3rd ceiling panel from starboard in the Pilot House

2 Wireless Chip Slots available. This device allows you to prioritize network connections. For example, you can make the Starlink network your first priority and a 2nd priority might be the marina WIFI, and a 3rd priority might be a wireless account (if you have installed the appropriate sim card in your Pepwave device. Further, you can install sim cards from 2 different wireless providers and if one provider does not have service, the Pepwave will automatically choose the backup wireless provider. Further, you can “bind” multiple network services together to combine their speed.

For Support, contact: Rob Cote @ 561-281-1626

Starlink: Router: SSID: N5524 Password: Nordhavn5524

Router is located in captains cabin closet. Antenna is located in port dome on the mast

For support contact: Rob Cote @ 561-281-1626

Acer Laptop Password: 5524

This device is used for backup navigation and for creating routes. When activated, the routes show up across all chart plotter devices.

Gost Security: Password: 5524

For support contact: info@gost.com or 954-565-9898

Currently includes: main salon door open, pilot house movement, smoke alarm, bilge alarm, loss of power on main 240v-50amp power source. There is a Gost remote FOB located just inside the salon door on the starboard side of the salon – look behind the wood trim along the ceiling.

Dynagen Auto-Start on 20KW Generator: Password: 0000

To access the maintenance panel on the Dynagen Auto-start, you will need to enter the password.

For support, contact: Cattron/Dynagen Support@cattron.zendesk.com or www.dynagen.com

Starlink

You will need to create an account to turn on Starlink. To do this, download the Starlink App on your phone, create and account, and link that account to your Starlink router/antenna. You’ll want to choose a level of service which will allow you to use the service while offshore and underway.

Dynagen Auto-Start

The Dynagen Auto-Start was installed on the 20KW generator for 2 reasons: (1) When in a marina, if the power goes out on the dock long enough for the batteries to discharge to a pre-set lower limit, the generator will come on and run long enough to recharge the batteries. (2) When at anchor, the system will keep the batteries charged without you having to think about charging.

The system is pretty seamless, but there are a couple of things to keep in mind:

  1. Starting/stopping the generator
  2. Make sure the Service Select is set to AUTO or 20KW Gen.
  3. Press the Start button on the control panel in the pilot house or from the control panel located on the back of the 20KW generator. When you press the START button, the Dynagen will heat the glow plugs for 10 seconds, then engage the starter. Once running Dynagen will not transfer load to the generator until a warm-up period has completed (about 1 minute).
  4. When you’re ready to stop the generator, press STOP on the control panel. Dynagen will remove the load from the generator and continue to run for about 2 minutes to allow the generator to cool down and then the generator will be stopped.
  5. Generator LOCK OUT - If you need to do any service to the generator, such as checking fluid levels, or changing oil, etc., you need to lock out the generator to keep it from starting automatically. There is a silver switch located below the control panel on the back of the generator. Switch it DOWN to shut down the Dynagen system, eliminating the possibility of an auto-start.
  6. Unexpected Shutdown – The generator has all of its original shutdown fail-safes in place, e.g., temp, oil pressure, exhaust temp, but Dynagen has further capabilities to manage its own shutdown scenarios. Those are defined in the system parameters which can be accessed from the control panel on the back of the engine. If the generator should shutdown unexpectedly, the first thing to do is check the system log on the Dynagen control panel. If Dynagen shut the generator down, you’ll be able to see exactly why. If, on the other hand, the fail-safes on the generator caused the shut down, you’ll likely get several error messages that don’t make sense like low RPM and low Oil pressure. Before proceeding, you’ll need to make sure that the engine didn’t overheat, run out of oil, etc.

If the normal suspects look ok on the engine, it might be something as simple as you overloaded the generator, causing it to bog down and die. If this happens, you will need to reset the generator at the control panel on the back of the generator. Press the STOP button, and then press the AUTO button. This will put the Dynagen back in its normal standby mode and you can start it from either control panel.

  1. Dynagen Configuration – If you want to change the settings on the Dynagen, you can access the settings from the control panel located on the rear of the generator, however the preferred method of configuration is to use the PC based configuration software. The backup navigation laptop (located in the Pilot House). Take the laptop with you to the engine room and use the USB cord attached to the control panel located at the back of the generator. The current and past configuration files are stored in a DYNAGEN folder located on the laptop’s desktop.

Backup Fresh Water Pump

There is a 24 Volt backup fresh water pump located below the main 120V pump in the equipment room. It would be a good idea to change to this pump one day a month to make sure it gets exercised. Follow the instructions below to change to the backup pump. Note: The backup pump will not come on immediately when you switch it on. Like the main pump, it only comes on when the water pressure falls below a certain level.

To change to the backup pump:

  1. At the electrical panel in the Pilot house, turn off the 120V pump
  2. There are two valves that have to be changed in the mechanical room:
  3. Upper Valve (closest to 120V pump)
  4. Turn the valve horizontal to the ceiling for the 120V pump
  5. Turn the valve vertical to the ceiling for the 24V pump

Figure : Upper fresh water pump valve

  1. Lower Valve (closest to the 24V pump)
  2. Turn the valve vertical to the ceiling for the 120V pump
  3. Turn the valve horizontal to the ceiling for the 24V pump

Figure : Lower fresh water pump valve

Air Conditioning Drain Assist

There are six (7) AC Condensers/fans located throughout the boat. Each condenser has a pan underneath it that captures condensation and all of the pans drain to a manifold that drains in to the grey water tank. Just like the condensation drains on your AC at home, occasionally the drains on the boat will get stopped up with algae and cause a drip of water below the condensation pans. On this boat, a prior owner/genius came up with a good solution. There is a hose spliced in to the manifold that is attached to a fresh water valve. When you turn on the valve, it creates a venturi that literally sucks all the water from the drain pans/hoses on each condensation pan. All you have to do is remember to turn on the valve for 10-15 seconds periodically and you’ll never have a problem with overflowing AC Condensation pans!

The valve is located underneath the sink in the master bathroom. We tried to remember to run the valve once a week when we were running the AC units.

Figure : Venturi Valve for AC's located under Sink in Master Bathroom

Anchor Washdown

The anchor washdown can be changed from salt water to fresh water by changing valve settings which are located in the forward end of the utility room.

To change to fresh water:

  1. Close the washdown thru hull which is located on port/forward in the equipment room
  2. Open the freshwater valve which is located on the ceiling in the forward equipment room
  3. Adjust the water transfer valve to the fresh water position located to starboard and below the Stingray pump in the equipment room.

To change output to salt water:

  1. Open the washdown thru hull
  2. Close the fresh water valve
  3. Adjust the water transfer valve to the salt water position

Figure : Washdown pump thru hull

Figure : Fresh water supply located on the ceiling in the forward equipment room

Figure : Fresh or Salt Water valve. UP for fresh, DOWN for salt.

Note on Anchor Washdown pump (Stingray Pump): If you are using the pump with salt water and if you don’t use it often (at least weekly?), the salt water left in the pump will dry and leave salt crystals behind which will jam the pump and you’ll have to turn the pump over manually to get it free and running again.

Here’s how to free the pump, if it locks up:

  1. Turn off the breaker for the pump which is located on the 110V panel in the Pilot House.
  2. Access the pump, located in the forward end of the equipment room.
  3. With a screwdriver, remove the fan shield located on the right side of the pump.
  4. Turn the fan blades until the pump turns freely.
  5. Replace the fan shield
  6. Turn on the power breaker

Figure : Stingray washdown pump. Fan cover is on the right side of the pump

Flushing the Water Makers

There are a couple of things to remember when you flush the water makers at the end of a water making session:

  1. Flush both water makers at the same time.
  2. Before you start the flush session, Turn off the Switch for the Media Filter.
  3. Open the fresh water valve located below the big water maker. See picture below (Figure 8).

Figure : Fresh Water Flush Valve

  1. Press the flush button on the big water maker.
  2. Turn on the small water maker (switch is located just below the light and fan switches on the aft side of the forward bulkhead in the equipment room).
  3. On both water makers, increase the pressure to approximately 200 PSI
  4. On the small water maker, you’ll need to put the bypass hose in a bucket so the water will not run in to the bilge.
  5. When the big water maker completes its flush cycle, reduce the pressure on the small water maker to zero and then turn off the small water maker.
  6. Close the fresh water flush valve
  7. Set the Media Filter switch back to AUTO

Media Filter

The Media Filter is intended to remove large particles from the water before they get to the primary (5 Micron) filter for the water maker. This is especially useful in areas where there is a lot of plankton in the water, such as the Pacific NW and parts of NE Canada. The media filter on N5524 is located on the port side in the forward equipment room. The filter contains sand and rock, similar to a pool filter.

On the front of the water maker there are a series of valves used to route water through the filter in different ways. The chart on the front of the filter will explain the correct positions to be used depending on what you are trying to accomplish.

The Media Filter is controlled by the 3-way switch that is located on the aft side of the forward bulkhead in the equipment room. The first position on the switch is AUTO. In that position, the Media Filter pump will come on when the large water maker’s Low Pressure pump is turned on. OFF is the center position and MANUAL is the 3rd position and turns the pump on, independent of the water maker. Use the MANUAL position to flush the media filter as needed (or monthly).

When using the big water maker, you do not need to do anything special for the media filter to operate properly. Leave the valves in the NORMAL OPERATION position and the switch in the AUTO position.

When running the small water maker, you will need to make a change to the control switch. Move the switch to the MANUAL position when you turn the water maker on and OFF or AUTO when you are done making water with the small water maker

When flushing the Water Makers, you will need to change the switch position to OFF and return the switch to AUTO when flushing is complete.

Ceiling Panel Removal

From time to time, you’ll find a need to remove one or more of the ceiling panels to access wires, equipment, etc. To remove the panels, you’ll want to get the panel removal tool that I made. It is located the drawer on the port side of the pilot house that includes some basic tools. Carefully slide the blade of the tool between the panel and the teak. Once you have the tool in place, push the handle of the tool back towards the ceiling and away from the panel you want to remove.

Figure : Panel removal tool

Thru-hull flush

Occasionally you may find that a jelly fish or plastic bag gets suck up against one of the thru-hulls, causing a system failure. For example, during the summer months while cruising the Chesapeake, you will notice tons of Jellyfish (they call them Nettles up there). Those nettles have a tendency to get sucked up in to the intake and the jelly mess will work its way all the way up to the strainer! The result is that you’ll get a “Low Pressure” error on the AC’s and they’ll quit cooling. Here’s an easy way to get the AC back up and running again without having to swim with the jellys to clear the thru-hull.

Note: You’ll find the strainer cap and hose needed to do this flush located next to the AC strainer

  1. Go to the AC strainer located in the hatch at the forward end of the 20KW Generator (below it).
  2. Find the valve located on the back side of the AC Strainer and close it. That will keep water from flowing to/from the AC Water Pump.
  3. Open the hatch on the engine room floor located just in front of the 20KW Generator and close the Generator Thru-hull.
  4. Put on a pair of rubber gloves.
  5. With the thru-hull off, open the top of the AC Strainer.
  6. Remove the strainer and place in a bucket or trash can. Most likely the strainer will be full of bits and pieces of jelly fish, so be careful not to get the bits on your skin.
  7. Rinse the strainer using the hose in the cockpit or on the swim step.
  8. Replace the strainer
  9. Rather than installing the normal screw top, use the top pictured below which has the hose bib installed on the top.
  10. Attach the short hose pictured below
  11. Attach the water hose located at the front of the port engine
  12. Open the AC strainer thru-hull
  13. Turn on the water hose and let it run for several minutes. Because you’ve closed the valve behind the strainer, the water will be forced down and through the thru-hull. This should clear the thru-hull screen of any remaining jellies, plastic bags, etc.
  14. Turn off the water and remove the temporary cap, replacing the original cap
  15. Open the valve located directly behind the strainer
  16. Open the AC Thru-hull valve.

Filling the boat with fuel and fuel levels per tank

When you fill the boat with fuel, following the notes located in the operations book in the Pilot House, you will notice a slight list to port. To correct this, consider not completely filling the port engine room tank. When filling the tank, stop about 70-80 gallons short of completely full. If you need all the fuel for a long run, you’ll have to live with the slight list until you can use 70-80 gallons of fuel while underway

One other odd issue that you should be aware. If you burn fuel from the forward center tank first, when the FC tank is below ½ tank, fuel will syphon from the ER Port tank up to the FC tank. The best way to deal with this is to run the tank down to ½ full and then close the valve located in the floor of the equipment room and manually transfer fuel from the forward center tank.

Anchoring

When anchoring, you will note that there are several wire ties attached to the chain at approximately every 25 feet. When you get ready to set the anchor, bring the boat to a stop and lower the anchor until it touches the bottom. Bump the boat in reverse just long enough to get the boat drifting back with the wind. Pay out the anchor, counting every time a group of wire ties goes by. 3 sets of wire ties = 75 feet, 4 sets = 100 feet, etc. I physically hold a hand out with the number of “sections” that have gone by until I reach my intended scope. This helps me remember where I am and lets the helmsman know how much chain is out.

Once you have let out adequate scope, let the boat slowly drift away from the anchor until it begins to pull tight. Here’s the important part: WAIT. If you try to back down on the anchor too soon, you’ll likely pull it free.

WAIT a couple of minutes and let the anchor settle naturally. WAIT another minute or two and then put one engine in reverse until the chain pulls tight. Next put the other engine in reverse and let it idle for a minute or two and the chain should pull tight. If you’ve got a good set, the anchor chain will remain tight with no skipping motions.

Now, slowly add throttle to the two engines until you feel the anchor is well set.

Finally install the snubber on the chain and let the chain out another 25-30 feet so the bridle is taking the load.

Flopper Stoppers

N5524 is equipped with flopper stoppers for the port and starboard side of the boat. Flopper Stoppers are designed to be deployed when anchored in a rolly anchorage. There are 2 white bags located in the lazarette that are marked PORT and STARBOARD. Each contains a flopper stopper (disassembled) and the lines required to set the device. All the lines are marked as to their purpose and are the exact length required for a safe and comfortable set. The instructions for putting the flopper stoppers together can be downloaded at www.flopstopper.com and there you’ll also see some valuable videos.

Assembly: Lay the side rails (U channel aluminum) out parallel about 3 feet apart and match the numbers up with the blades, and the locking pieces. Take a look at the video on the company website. I added the numbers on the parts to make it easier to remember where everything goes.

Setting the Flopper Stoppers:

  1. Set the topping lift on the eye located on the mast (port and starboard).
  2. Attach one end of the forward guy, aft guy, and Flopper Stopper line to the boom. The FS line should be attached on the one tang that is straight.
  3. Attach the topping lift to the tang just opposite of the FS line
  4. Make sure someone is holding on to the aft guy and the FS Line
  5. Using the forward guy, pull the boom out until you can snap the shackle in to the forward eye. The topping lift should be holding the boom up perpendicular to the water.
  6. The next step is to attach the aft guy to the aft tang. Because the lines are designed to be tight when attached, you may find it difficult to snap the shackle on to the aft tang. I’ve found that if you get someone to go up to the boat deck and pull up on the topping lift about 3-4 feet, you’ll have enough slack in the aft guy to easily attach the snap shackle to aft tang. If you don’t have a helper, temporarily tie the topping lift higher than it would normally be and then after the aft guy is attached, let it back out.
  7. Attach the FS Line to the bridle on the flopper stopper.
  8. Make sure you have hold of the retrieval line.
  9. If you’re deploying the starboard FS, you will launch it from the starboard boarding door. If you’re deploying the port FS, you’ll launch it from the swim platform.

A couple of notes:

  1. If you think you need one flopper stopper, put them both out. There is a huge difference between one and two when the bay is rough!
  2. Once we’ve deployed the FS’s the first time and we’re moving short distances, we pull the FS out of the water and tie them standing on their side in the cockpit. Even lazier, if we’re only traveling a short distance between anchorages and the weather is benign, we often leave the booms out (like a shrimp boat) with the flopper stopper line tied so it doesn’t drag in the water. The only time we put everything away is if we’re going to a marina, of if we have an extended run to make between anchorages.

I would recommend that you anchor out in some comfortable spot and practice putting them together and setting them. The first time or two may be a bit frustrating, but with practice, you’ll be able to get them set within 10-15 minutes.

Cockpit Shades

There are 2 covers located in the aft starboard locker (below the wing station in the cockpit). One is Phifertex which is semi-transparent (but not waterproof. The other is made from Stamoid and is waterproof. Both have a bolt rope sewn in to the top and go in to the Rail Track which runs across the underside of the boat deck at the stern.

Main Salon Shades

The shades in the main salon should be open when underway offshore.

About Clark Haley

Clark is a licensed Florida yacht broker with One Water Yacht Sales, based in Jupiter, Florida. He specializes in motor and sail yachts in the 50-80 foot range and represents the full Riviera Yachts line.
